question: Based on Figure 1.2-3 (NPM with secondary equipment) and Figure 5.4-1 (SG assembly), is the interface between primary and secondary systems adequately designed?

nuscale_ch05_0020.title: Steam Generators
nuscale_ch05_0020.summary: Each NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel contains two integral steam generators, which are part of the reactor coolant system and share the vessel’s pressure boundary. The steam generator system includes feedwater and main steam piping, thermal relief valves, and access ports, with the reactor pressure vessel itself forming the shell and pressure boundary for the steam generators.

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems in the NuScale Power Module, detailing the integration of the reactor, steam generators, pressurizer, safety and ECCS valves, and associated piping within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). It also outlines the configuration and boundaries of the steam generator system, emphasizing the integral design of the steam generators within the RPV and the separation between primary and secondary sides.

nuscale_ch01_0016.title: Principal Site Characteristics
nuscale_ch01_0016.summary: This section provides a general overview of the NuScale plant design, outlining key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, cooling, and waste management. It explains that each Combined License (COL) applicant must reference the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) and supplement it with site-specific information, while distinguishing between certified design elements and conceptual design information (CDI). Security-related details are specially marked and withheld according to regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0015.title: General Plant Description
nuscale_ch01_0015.summary: This section provides a summary of the NuScale plant’s overall design, including key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, cooling water, waste management, and auxiliary systems. It explains that each COL applicant must reference the NuScale FSAR and provide site-specific details, with certain conceptual design information and security-related content clearly identified and delineated. Figures referenced in the section illustrate the scope of certified and site-specific designs, as well as the general site layout.

nuscale_ch01_0017.title: Facility Description
nuscale_ch01_0017.summary: The Facility Description section outlines the NuScale plant’s cooling and power generation processes. It explains that the reactor core heats coolant, which circulates naturally to transfer heat to steam generators, producing steam that drives a turbine and generates electricity. The section also notes that site-specific details, such as cooling tower design and operation, are addressed elsewhere in the report.

nuscale_ch01_0782.title: SRP 15.6.5.B, Rev 1: Radiological Consequences of a Design Basis Loss-of-Coolant Accident: Leakage From Engineered Safety Feature Components Outside ContainmentII.1 ESF System Leakage Assumptions
nuscale_ch01_0782.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for evaluating radiological consequences of a design basis loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA), specifically regarding leakage from engineered safety feature (ESF) components outside containment. It notes that NuScale fully conforms to the ESF system leakage assumptions in SRP 15.6.5.B, Rev 1, while certain other guidance areas only partially apply due to superseding criteria in SRP Section 15.0.3 and applicability to specific reactor types.

nuscale_ch01_0653.title: DSRS 15.0, Rev 0: Introduction - Transient and Accident AnalysesI.8.C Core, System, and Barrier Performance
nuscale_ch01_0653.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for transient and accident analyses, noting partial or non-applicability where NuScale’s design differs from traditional BWRs or uses alternative methodologies (such as AST for radiological consequences). It highlights that NuScale evaluates critical heat flux (CHF) instead of DNBR, supports exemptions from certain 10 CFR 50 Appendix K requirements, and only addresses technically relevant features in its ECCS evaluation models.

nuscale_ch01_0071.title: Interfaces with Certified Design
nuscale_ch01_0071.summary: This section explains the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant’s certified design and the site-specific design provided in the combined license (COL) application. It describes the types of interface requirements (conceptual design information and COL information items), identifies which structures and systems are included in the certified design, and outlines the responsibilities of the COL applicant, including providing information on departures from the certified design. Tables and figures referenced in the section help clarify these boundaries and requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0118.title: 1.8 Interfaces with Certified Design
nuscale_ch01_0118.summary: This section explains the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant’s certified design and the site-specific design provided in the combined license (COL) application. It distinguishes between conceptual design information (CDI) for non-certified portions and COL information items, which are site-specific responsibilities. The section also notes that COL applicants must address all COL information items and provide a list of any departures from the certified design.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) does not use traditional components with designed leakage, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using current instrumentation. Any leakage into containment, regardless of source, quickly vaporizes and disperses, so all such leakage is treated as unidentified until specifically located and quantified by other means. The RCS leakage rate is determined by inventory balance, and the detection systems are designed to be reliable and sensitive enough to support safety analyses.

nuscale_ch01_0658.title: SRP 15.0.2, (March 2007): Review of Transient and Accident Analysis MethodsII.3 Code Assessment Departure
nuscale_ch01_0658.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for transient and accident analysis methods. It notes that NuScale’s design partially conforms to certain guidance, using critical heat flux (CHF) instead of DNBR and a modified alternative source term (AST) methodology for radiological consequences. Additionally, NuScale’s ECCS evaluation models for LOCAs address only the technically relevant features required by regulations, supporting exemptions from some requirements in 10 CFR 50 Appendix K.

nuscale_ch05_0011.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing
nuscale_ch05_0011.summary: This section describes the materials and standards used for threaded fasteners in key reactor coolant system components, emphasizing the use of Alloy 718 for its compatibility and stress corrosion cracking resistance. It also outlines the requirements for preservice and inservice inspection and testing of reactor coolant pressure boundary components, specifying adherence to the latest ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) editions as mandated by regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0007.title: Integrity of Reactor Coolant Boundary
nuscale_ch05_0007.summary: The NuScale Power Module (NPM) is designed to maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) throughout the plant’s life, in accordance with regulatory definitions. The RCPB includes all pressure-retaining components of the reactor coolant system up to key isolation valves and safety systems, encompassing the reactor pressure vessel, associated piping, valves, and connected systems. These design features ensure the RCPB’s reliability and compliance with safety standards.

nuscale_ch01_0451.title: BTP 10-2, Rev 4: Design Guidelines for Avoiding Water Hammers in Steam GeneratorsPSGD B.1 thru B.4Preheat Steam Generator Designs
nuscale_ch01_0451.summary: This section explains that the NuScale plant design does not use preheat steam generator designs, so the regulatory guidelines for avoiding water hammers in such systems are not applicable. The NuScale design differs from conventional large PWRs and does not require the specific measures outlined in BTP 10-2, Rev 4 for preheat steam generators.

nuscale_ch01_0452.title: BTP 10-2, Rev 4: Design Guidelines for Avoiding Water Hammers in Steam GeneratorsOTSGD B.1 Once-Through Steam Generator Designs - Auxiliary Feedwater Supply
nuscale_ch01_0452.summary: This section explains that the NuScale plant design does not use the steam generator configurations (top-feed, preheat, or once-through with auxiliary feedwater) addressed by BTP 10-2, Rev 4, and therefore the associated water hammer guidelines are not applicable. Instead, NuScale uses a passive Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) that fulfills a similar safety function without the need for auxiliary feedwater pumps or external supply headers, inherently avoiding the water hammer concerns targeted by these guidelines.

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information. It lists the relevant diagrams and legends used for both I&C and piping and instrumentation diagrams, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by COL applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch05_0021.title: Reactor Coolant System Piping
nuscale_ch05_0021.summary: The section discusses the design and degradation assessment of the NuScale Reactor Coolant System's steam generator (SG) tubing. It notes that the primary degradation observed in similar operating reactors is wear from flow-induced vibration or foreign objects, and that NuScale's design—with its helical tubing and novel support structure—does not increase risk compared to existing designs. Prototypic testing and standard SG program guidelines are deemed sufficient, with wear identified as the most likely degradation mechanism and corrosion risks assessed as low.

nuscale_ch01_0072.title: Combined License Information Items
nuscale_ch01_0072.summary: This section outlines the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant’s certified design and the site-specific design provided in the Combined License (COL) application. It explains the types of interface requirements (Conceptual Design Information and COL information items), and describes how COL applicants must address site-specific information and any departures from the certified design. Tables referenced in the section summarize these interfaces and list the required COL information items.
